There will likely be a slew of developments out of the Middle East with the US blockade of Iran along with US/Israel/Lebanon negotiations (which are due to take place in Washington this week). The IMF/World Bank Spring meetings take place in Washington (4/13-18). For US earnings , the key reports include: Mon morning ( GS ); Tues morning ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX , and WFC ); Wed morning ( BAC, PNC , and MS ); Wed night ( SLG ); Thurs morning ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, SCHW, TRV , TSM , and USB ); Thurs night ( AA and NFLX ); and Fri morning ( RF, STT , and TFC ). In Europe, the key earnings reports include: Mon night ( LVMH ), Tues night ( Kering ); Wed morning ( ASML and Hermes ); Thurs morning ( Entain and Pernod Ricard ); and Fri morning ( Ericsson ).

source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Monday April 13, 2026
market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are well off their overnight lows : S&P -38 points/55bp to 6816 (the overnight low was 6767), Dow -226 points/47bp, Nasdaq -160 points/63bp, and R2 K -23 points/92bp. The major Eurozone indices are down 70-100bp , with underperformance in autos, industrials, tech, banks, travel, and retail while utilities, energy, real estate, media, defense contractors, insurance, staples, and chemicals outperform. Energy is the only major sub-group seeing outright gains (the SXEP is up ~90bp). Stocks in Asia saw mixed price action on Monday: TPX -0.45%, NKY -0.74%, Hang Seng -0.9%, HSCEI -0.61%, SHCOMP +0.06%, Shenzhen +0.54%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.11%, Korea KOSPI -0.86%, Australia ASX 200 -0.39%, and India is down ~75bp. Treasuries are seeing modest pressure , with yields up 2-3bp across the curve. The market is pricing in essentially no movement on the Funds Rate for the balance of the year. The DXY is rallying 32bp (it finished in the red on each day last week). Brent is jumping ~770bp to ~$102.50 (it hasn’t fully returned to the pre-ceasefire high of ~$109.30, but it’s well off the recent trough of ~$90). Gold is down ~60bp while silver drops 205bp. Bitcoin fell 270bp on Sun and is off another 90bp this morning (to ~$70.72 K).
•
whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – the equity declines (at least so far) aren’t as bad as some would have thought based on the tenor of media reporting out of Pakistan over the weekend. Our view is that while the meeting was certainly disappointing , it was hardly catastrophic , and if one looks closely, Trump seems to be pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation (in that his appetite for a resumption of the aggressive bombing campaign of the last several weeks, much less a ground troop deployment , appears to have dropped significantly ). The Hormuz blockage in reality isn’t as draconian as it initially appeared – the US Navy is focused on interdicting ships traveling to/from Iranian ports, not all vessels moving through the waterway . The situation remains very fluid and fraught with risk , and the blockade could easily trigger an unplanned escalation. And one can make the case that Trump is trapped – he can’t escalate or exit (and he’s not fully in charge of the entire situation with the Lebanon war continuing). However, we think the conflict remains on a trajectory of resolution (or at least a sustained détente similar to the aftermath of the first Iraq war) , and it’s likely there will be ongoing diplomacy in the days (perhaps hours) ahead . The nuclear/enrichment issue seems fairly “easy” to solve as the JCPOA provided a template acceptable to both sides 10 years ago (the US can acknowledge a rich to enrich while Iran can foreswear enriching). For a full recap of our Iran-related content from the weekend , see here and here (a lot of this is reprinted below too). While our view on the war remains relatively sanguine (compared to the consensus), with the SPX bumping up at the ~6900-7000 pre-war resistance zone , the upside potential isn’t enormous , at least for now, and there will be significant economic fallout from what’s already occurred . Aside from geopolitics, we didn’t see any other news items that were that important (although these two articles are interesting for the AI conversation: “ AI Is Using So Much Energy That Computing Firepower Is Running Out ” from the WSJ and “ Anthropic's Claude Mythos isn't a sentient super-hacker, it's a sales pitch ” from Tom’s Hardware ).
• What we’ll be watching on Monday – we care most about Goldman earnings before the US open, the start of the US Hormuz blockade (it’s set to go into effect at 10am ET), and LVMH earnings after the EU close.
• What we’ll be watching during the week of Monday 4/13 – it’s likely the Middle East will continue dominating the narrative as the world watches to see how the US blockade of Iranian traffic through Hormuz works (and whether the two sides hold additional talks ) while officials from Lebanon, the US, and Israel sit down (which they are scheduled to do in Washington). In addition, the CQ1 earnings season starts in earnest , with results due from US banks (BAC, C, GS, JPM, MS, WFC, and others), European luxury firms (Hermes, Kering, and LVMH), and a few prominent tech companies (ASML, NFLX, and TSMC).
• Market in a Minute for Monday (macro) - AI compute crisis: the AI industry is experiencing a massive compute shortage that’s forcing firms like Anthropic to impose significant limits on how users can access certain products ( WSJ ); Tax cheating on the rise amid a sharp reduction in IRS staffing (IRS enforcement worker headcount is down by ~1/3 from the Biden-era peak) ( WSJ ); BOJ’s Ueda signals the central bank is unlikely to tighten policy in the immediate term given ongoing uncertainty stemming from the Middle East war ( Bloomberg )

US/Iran meeting in Pakistan: bull vs. bear takeaways

• What bulls are saying – the fact the meeting took place at all is a sign of progress . The highest-level contact between US and Iranian officials in nearly half a century occurred just days after a brutal war, and talks stretched on for nearly 24 hours . Trump Sunday morning wrote that “ the meeting went well ” and that “ most points were agreed to ” (a Washington Post report at ~4:30pm ET said the Pakistan meeting might not have yielded a deal , but it did help foster an environment of goodwill, which has led the White House to feel that Iran “may still come to accept their terms” ). The last major deal between the US and Iran, the Obama-era nuclear agreement, took two years to reach , so to think a full accord could be struck after just a weekend was unrealistic (it seems like the US and Iran reached agreement on certain topics during the weekend negotiations, and a Reuters report made it sound like Washington was prepared to release billions of frozen Iranian funds ). The current ceasefire (which continues to hold) still has more time (it ends on Apr 21), mediators (especially Pakistan) are working furiously behind the scenes to sustain the détente, and communications are ongoing between the two sides (a spokesman for Iran’s foreign ministry released the following statement: “we should never have expected to reach a deal in one session. We will continue to work to bring the two views of Americans and Iranians closer together”. A diplomat quoted in the FT said more negotiations were expected , but nothing had been scheduled). While the war in Lebanon is ongoing, Israel has dialed back the intensity of its strikes and seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut (officials from Israel, Lebanon, and the US are set to meet this week ). Two US Navy warships entered Hormuz over the weekend for the first time since the war commenced as part of an initiative to sweep for mines , and Saudi Arabia said it has fully restored its East-West pipeline (which bypassed Hormuz) to ~7 M BPD. Trump’s options for escalation in Iran remain awful (which is why he’s unlikely to take them): destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure , militarily (i.e. with troops) reopening Hormuz (which would involve putting tens, if not hundreds of thousands, of soldiers on the ground for an extended period), and/or seizing Iran’s enriched uranium (which could be even more complex than using troops to guarantee Hormuz passage). His decision to impose a blockade of Hormuz (announced Sunday morning) isn’t ideal , but it’s better than a dramatic escalation in the military campaign (the Pentagon issued a statement clarifying Trump’s blockade proclamation, and the language seemed to soften what the president posted earlier Sun morning – the US will block any ships “entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas” but will permit other boats to transit the waterway ). According to a WSJ article Sunday night, Trump is unlikely to resume a full bombing campaign against Iran , which means the odds of a ground troop deployment is very low , as he grows increasingly concerned about further destabilizing the region (this was echoed by a separate Washington Post article – “ Trump has no appetite for further armed conflict ”).
• What bears are saying – the talks in Pakistan broke down over stark disagreements over critical and fundamental issues , including Hormuz control, the existing stockpile of enriched uranium, and Iran’s right to enrich. Vance said the US outlined its “best and final” offer , suggesting Washington wasn’t interested in negotiating further (either Iran can take what’s on the table or not), and Netanyahu told the Israeli public over the weekend that “ the battle is not yet over ” regarding Iran . A trio of WSJ articles published over the weekend suggested the recent military campaign against Iran largely failed to achieve its key objectives ( these reports, if accurate, are even more negative than the lack of a deal in Pakistan ): “ The U.S. Sank One of Iran’s Navies. The Other Still Controls Hormuz ” ( WSJ ), “ Iran Has Thousands of Missiles and Could Retrieve Launchers, U.S. Intelligence Finds ” ( WSJ ), and “ Iran’s Nuclear Program Has Survived, Clouding Talks ” ( WSJ ). Iran’s regime remains firmly entrenched , and the country still possesses a capability and will to wage a campaign of economic warfare against critical assets in the region (the damage inflicted to key energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and elsewhere, is already substantial ). Tehran doesn’t need to expend much energy to keep Hormuz closed , and ships will avoid passing until Iran firmly commits to freedom of navigation (a NYT report over the weekend claimed that one of the big problems is that Iran can’t locate all the mines it laid during the war ). Trump’s decision to impose a Hormuz blockade risks triggering escalation (Trump warned that “ any Iranian who fires on us, or at peaceful vessels, will be blown to hell ” while Iran said any military vessels approaching the waterway would be considered a ceasefire violation ). According to a WSJ report Sunday night, the US could resume limited military strikes against Iran .
• How we’re thinking about the situation – the Pakistan meeting was definitely a disappointment for US equities (especially considering the recent rally and the fact the SPX had rebounded back to the pre-war resistance zone of ~6900-7000), but we don’t think it will trigger an immediate collapse of the ceasefire (which still seems to be holding as of Mon morning) or an escalation in fighting (our core view remains that the escalatory options available to Trump are so bad that they remain unlikely, which is why he decided to impose a Hormuz blockade rather than launch a fresh round of strikes and/or a ground troop deployment ). Agreement was apparently reached on certain points (which Trump acknowledged Sunday morning – “ the meeting went well...most points were agreed to ”), and neither side is ruling out further talks . Iran has made commitments in the recent past not to pursue a nuclear weapon (this was a cornerstone of the 2015 nuclear agreement) and it wouldn’t take much for it to do so again , while its enriched uranium seems largely inaccessible . The IDF seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut , and it’s possible the talks between Israel, Lebanon, and the US this week (scheduled to occur in Washington) could yield further progress (which could help the US-Iran relationship ).

Data/monetary policy recap from Friday morning

• What bulls were talking about : the Michigan sentiment report was bad , but 1) 98% of the interviews took place before the recent ceasefire announcement and 2) long-term inflation expectations (which the Fed cares about most) were fairly muted. The Mar CPI wasn’t as bad as feared , with the core CPI only accelerating 10bp to +2.6% (vs. the Street +2.7%).
• What bears were talking about : the US CPI for Mar wasn’t as bad as feared, but inflation is still running very hot at +3.3% headline and +2.6% core (and the core number will face further upside pressure in the months ahead as it will take some time for the recent energy price spike to seep further into the broader economy). The Michigan sentiment survey for Apr was net negative , with a sharp deterioration in Expectations (down >5 points M/M to 46.1) and a big short-term inflation jump (1-year inflation expectations rose 100bp to +4.8% vs. +3.8% in Mar). The inability of Treasuries to rally at all this week is a giant red flag about the inflationary implications of the war. San Francisco Fed President Daly warned in a Reuters interview that the Iran war-driven oil shock will make it even harder for US inflation to return to the 2% target (“We had work to do before we had the oil price shock; with the oil price shock, the work just takes longer”). China’s PPI had been a major source of global disinflation , but those days might be coming to an end based on the Mar data (the PPI flipped into positive territory for the first time in more than three years).

And one can make the case that Trump is trapped – he can’t escalate or exit (and he’s not fully in charge of the entire situation with the Lebanon war continuing). However, we think the conflict remains on a trajectory of resolution (or at least a sustained détente similar to the aftermath of the first Iraq war) , and it’s likely there will be ongoing diplomacy in the days (perhaps hours) ahead . The nuclear/enrichment issue seems fairly “easy” to solve as the JCPOA provided a template acceptable to both sides 10 years ago (the US can acknowledge a rich to enrich while Iran can foreswear enriching). For a full recap of our Iran-related content from the weekend , see here and here (a lot of this is reprinted below too). While our view on the war remains relatively sanguine (compared to the consensus), with the SPX bumping up at the ~6900-7000 pre-war resistance zone , the upside potential isn’t enormous , at least for now, and there will be significant economic fallout from what’s already occurred . Aside from geopolitics, we didn’t see any other news items that were that important (although these two articles are interesting for the AI conversation: “ AI Is Using So Much Energy That Computing Firepower Is Running Out ” from the WSJ and “ Anthropic's Claude Mythos isn't a sentient super-hacker, it's a sales pitch ” from Tom’s Hardware ).

US/Iran meeting in Pakistan: bull vs. bear takeaways

• What bulls are saying – the fact the meeting took place at all is a sign of progress . The highest-level contact between US and Iranian officials in nearly half a century occurred just days after a brutal war, and talks stretched on for nearly 24 hours . Trump Sunday morning wrote that “ the meeting went well ” and that “ most points were agreed to ” (a Washington Post report at ~4:30pm ET said the Pakistan meeting might not have yielded a deal , but it did help foster an environment of goodwill, which has led the White House to feel that Iran “may still come to accept their terms” ). The last major deal between the US and Iran, the Obama-era nuclear agreement, took two years to reach , so to think a full accord could be struck after just a weekend was unrealistic (it seems like the US and Iran reached agreement on certain topics during the weekend negotiations, and a Reuters report made it sound like Washington was prepared to release billions of frozen Iranian funds ). The current ceasefire (which continues to hold) still has more time (it ends on Apr 21), mediators (especially Pakistan) are working furiously behind the scenes to sustain the détente, and communications are ongoing between the two sides (a spokesman for Iran’s foreign ministry released the following statement: “we should never have expected to reach a deal in one session. We will continue to work to bring the two views of Americans and Iranians closer together”. A diplomat quoted in the FT said more negotiations were expected , but nothing had been scheduled). While the war in Lebanon is ongoing, Israel has dialed back the intensity of its strikes and seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut (officials from Israel, Lebanon, and the US are set to meet this week ). Two US Navy warships entered Hormuz over the weekend for the first time since the war commenced as part of an initiative to sweep for mines , and Saudi Arabia said it has fully restored its East-West pipeline (which bypassed Hormuz) to ~7 M BPD. Trump’s options for escalation in Iran remain awful (which is why he’s unlikely to take them): destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure , militarily (i.e. with troops) reopening Hormuz (which would involve putting tens, if not hundreds of thousands, of soldiers on the ground for an extended period), and/or seizing Iran’s enriched uranium (which could be even more complex than using troops to guarantee Hormuz passage). His decision to impose a blockade of Hormuz (announced Sunday morning) isn’t ideal , but it’s better than a dramatic escalation in the military campaign (the Pentagon issued a statement clarifying Trump’s blockade proclamation, and the language seemed to soften what the president posted earlier Sun morning – the US will block any ships “entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas” but will permit other boats to transit the waterway ). According to a WSJ article Sunday night, Trump is unlikely to resume a full bombing campaign against Iran , which means the odds of a ground troop deployment is very low , as he grows increasingly concerned about further destabilizing the region (this was echoed by a separate Washington Post article – “ Trump has no appetite for further armed conflict ”).
• What bears are saying – the talks in Pakistan broke down over stark disagreements over critical and fundamental issues , including Hormuz control, the existing stockpile of enriched uranium, and Iran’s right to enrich. Vance said the US outlined its “best and final” offer , suggesting Washington wasn’t interested in negotiating further (either Iran can take what’s on the table or not), and Netanyahu told the Israeli public over the weekend that “ the battle is not yet over ” regarding Iran . A trio of WSJ articles published over the weekend suggested the recent military campaign against Iran largely failed to achieve its key objectives ( these reports, if accurate, are even more negative than the lack of a deal in Pakistan ): “ The U.S. Sank One of Iran’s Navies. The Other Still Controls Hormuz ” ( WSJ ), “ Iran Has Thousands of Missiles and Could Retrieve Launchers, U.S. Intelligence Finds ” ( WSJ ), and “ Iran’s Nuclear Program Has Survived, Clouding Talks ” ( WSJ ). Iran’s regime remains firmly entrenched , and the country still possesses a capability and will to wage a campaign of economic warfare against critical assets in the region (the damage inflicted to key energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and elsewhere, is already substantial ). Tehran doesn’t need to expend much energy to keep Hormuz closed , and ships will avoid passing until Iran firmly commits to freedom of navigation (a NYT report over the weekend claimed that one of the big problems is that Iran can’t locate all the mines it laid during the war ). Trump’s decision to impose a Hormuz blockade risks triggering escalation (Trump warned that “ any Iranian who fires on us, or at peaceful vessels, will be blown to hell ” while Iran said any military vessels approaching the waterway would be considered a ceasefire violation ). According to a WSJ report Sunday night, the US could resume limited military strikes against Iran .
• How we’re thinking about the situation – the Pakistan meeting was definitely a disappointment for US equities (especially considering the recent rally and the fact the SPX had rebounded back to the pre-war resistance zone of ~6900-7000), but we don’t think it will trigger an immediate collapse of the ceasefire (which still seems to be holding as of Mon morning) or an escalation in fighting (our core view remains that the escalatory options available to Trump are so bad that they remain unlikely, which is why he decided to impose a Hormuz blockade rather than launch a fresh round of strikes and/or a ground troop deployment ). Agreement was apparently reached on certain points (which Trump acknowledged Sunday morning – “ the meeting went well...most points were agreed to ”), and neither side is ruling out further talks . Iran has made commitments in the recent past not to pursue a nuclear weapon (this was a cornerstone of the 2015 nuclear agreement) and it wouldn’t take much for it to do so again , while its enriched uranium seems largely inaccessible . The IDF seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut , and it’s possible the talks between Israel, Lebanon, and the US this week (scheduled to occur in Washington) could yield further progress (which could help the US-Iran relationship ).

Data/monetary policy recap from Friday morning

• What bulls were talking about : the Michigan sentiment report was bad , but 1) 98% of the interviews took place before the recent ceasefire announcement and 2) long-term inflation expectations (which the Fed cares about most) were fairly muted. The Mar CPI wasn’t as bad as feared , with the core CPI only accelerating 10bp to +2.6% (vs. the Street +2.7%).
• What bears were talking about : the US CPI for Mar wasn’t as bad as feared, but inflation is still running very hot at +3.3% headline and +2.6% core (and the core number will face further upside pressure in the months ahead as it will take some time for the recent energy price spike to seep further into the broader economy). The Michigan sentiment survey for Apr was net negative , with a sharp deterioration in Expectations (down >5 points M/M to 46.1) and a big short-term inflation jump (1-year inflation expectations rose 100bp to +4.8% vs. +3.8% in Mar). The inability of Treasuries to rally at all this week is a giant red flag about the inflationary implications of the war. San Francisco Fed President Daly warned in a Reuters interview that the Iran war-driven oil shock will make it even harder for US inflation to return to the 2% target (“We had work to do before we had the oil price shock; with the oil price shock, the work just takes longer”). China’s PPI had been a major source of global disinflation , but those days might be coming to an end based on the Mar data (the PPI flipped into positive territory for the first time in more than three years).

energy_section:
energy, real estate, media, defense contractors, insurance, staples, and chemicals outperform. Energy is the only major sub-group seeing outright gains (the SXEP is up ~90bp). Stocks in Asia saw mixed price action on Monday: TPX -0.45%, NKY -0.74%, Hang Seng -0.9%, HSCEI -0.61%, SHCOMP +0.06%, Shenzhen +0.54%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.11%, Korea KOSPI -0.86%, Australia ASX 200 -0.39%, and India is down ~75bp. Treasuries are seeing modest pressure , with yields up 2-3bp across the curve. The market is pricing in essentially no movement on the Funds Rate for the balance of the year. The DXY is rallying 32bp (it finished in the red on each day last week). Brent is jumping ~770bp to ~$102.50 (it hasn’t fully returned to the pre-ceasefire high of ~$109.30, but it’s well off the recent trough of ~$90). Gold is down ~60bp while silver drops 205bp. Bitcoin fell 270bp on Sun and is off another 90bp this morning (to ~$70.72 K).
• What’s happening this morning – the equity declines (at least so far) aren’t as bad as some would have thought based on the tenor of media reporting out of Pakistan over the weekend. Our view is that while the meeting was certainly disappointing , it was hardly catastrophic , and if one looks closely, Trump seems to be pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation (in that his appetite for a resumption of the aggressive bombing campaign of the last several weeks, much less a ground troop deployment , appears to have dropped significantly ). The Hormuz blockage in reality isn’t as draconian as it initially appeared – the US Navy is focused on interdicting ships traveling to/from Iranian ports, not all vessels moving through the waterway . The situation remains very fluid and fraught with risk , and the blockade could easily trigger an unplanned escalation. And one can make the case that Trump is trapped – he can’t escalate or exit (and he’s not fully in charge of the entire situation with the Lebanon war continuing). However, we think the conflict remains on a trajectory of resolution (or at least a sustained détente similar to the aftermath of the first Iraq war) , and it’s likely there will be ongoing diplomacy in the days (perhaps hours) ahead . The nuclear/enrichment issue seems fairly “easy” to solve as the JCPOA provided a template acceptable to both sides 10 years ago (the US can acknowledge a rich to enrich while Iran can foreswear enriching). For a full recap of our Iran-related content from the weekend , see here and here (a lot of this is reprinted below too). While our view on the war remains relatively sanguine (compared to the consensus), with the SPX bumping up at the ~6900-7000 pre-war resistance zone , the upside potential isn’t enormous , at least for now, and there will be significant economic fallout from what’s already occurred . Aside from geopolitics, we didn’t see any other news items that were that important (although these two articles are interesting for the AI conversation: “ AI Is Using So Much Energy That Computing Firepower Is Running Out ” from the WSJ and “ Anthropic's Claude Mythos isn't a sentient super-hacker, it's a sales pitch ” from Tom’s Hardware ).
• What we’ll be watching on Monday – we care most about Goldman earnings before the US open, the start of the US Hormuz blockade (it’s set to go into effect at 10am ET), and LVMH earnings after the EU close.

US/Iran meeting in Pakistan: bull vs. bear takeaways

• What bulls are saying – the fact the meeting took place at all is a sign of progress . The highest-level contact between US and Iranian officials in nearly half a century occurred just days after a brutal war, and talks stretched on for nearly 24 hours . Trump Sunday morning wrote that “ the meeting went well ” and that “ most points were agreed to ” (a Washington Post report at ~4:30pm ET said the Pakistan meeting might not have yielded a deal , but it did help foster an environment of goodwill, which has led the White House to feel that Iran “may still come to accept their terms” ). The last major deal between the US and Iran, the Obama-era nuclear agreement, took two years to reach , so to think a full accord could be struck after just a weekend was unrealistic (it seems like the US and Iran reached agreement on certain topics during the weekend negotiations, and a Reuters report made it sound like Washington was prepared to release billions of frozen Iranian funds ). The current ceasefire (which continues to hold) still has more time (it ends on Apr 21), mediators (especially Pakistan) are working furiously behind the scenes to sustain the détente, and communications are ongoing between the two sides (a spokesman for Iran’s foreign ministry released the following statement: “we should never have expected to reach a deal in one session. We will continue to work to bring the two views of Americans and Iranians closer together”. A diplomat quoted in the FT said more negotiations were expected , but nothing had been scheduled). While the war in Lebanon is ongoing, Israel has dialed back the intensity of its strikes and seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut (officials from Israel, Lebanon, and the US are set to meet this week ). Two US Navy warships entered Hormuz over the weekend for the first time since the war commenced as part of an initiative to sweep for mines , and Saudi Arabia said it has fully restored its East-West pipeline (which bypassed Hormuz) to ~7 M BPD. Trump’s options for escalation in Iran remain awful (which is why he’s unlikely to take them): destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure , militarily (i.e. with troops) reopening Hormuz (which would involve putting tens, if not hundreds of thousands, of soldiers on the ground for an extended period), and/or seizing Iran’s enriched uranium (which could be even more complex than using troops to guarantee Hormuz passage). His decision to impose a blockade of Hormuz (announced Sunday morning) isn’t ideal , but it’s better than a dramatic escalation in the military campaign (the Pentagon issued a statement clarifying Trump’s blockade proclamation, and the language seemed to soften what the president posted earlier Sun morning – the US will block any ships “entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas” but will permit other boats to transit the waterway ). According to a WSJ article Sunday night, Trump is unlikely to resume a full bombing campaign against Iran , which means the odds of a ground troop deployment is very low , as he grows increasingly concerned about further destabilizing the region (this was echoed by a separate Washington Post article – “ Trump has no appetite for further armed conflict ”).
• What bears are saying – the talks in Pakistan broke down over stark disagreements over critical and fundamental issues , including Hormuz control, the existing stockpile of enriched uranium, and Iran’s right to enrich. Vance said the US outlined its “best and final” offer , suggesting Washington wasn’t interested in negotiating further (either Iran can take what’s on the table or not), and Netanyahu told the Israeli public over the weekend that “ the battle is not yet over ” regarding Iran . A trio of WSJ articles published over the weekend suggested the recent military campaign against Iran largely failed to achieve its key objectives ( these reports, if accurate, are even more negative than the lack of a deal in Pakistan ): “ The U.S. Sank One of Iran’s Navies. The Other Still Controls Hormuz ” ( WSJ ), “ Iran Has Thousands of Missiles and Could Retrieve Launchers, U.S. Intelligence Finds ” ( WSJ ), and “ Iran’s Nuclear Program Has Survived, Clouding Talks ” ( WSJ ). Iran’s regime remains firmly entrenched , and the country still possesses a capability and will to wage a campaign of economic warfare against critical assets in the region (the damage inflicted to key energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and elsewhere, is already substantial ). Tehran doesn’t need to expend much energy to keep Hormuz closed , and ships will avoid passing until Iran firmly commits to freedom of navigation (a NYT report over the weekend claimed that one of the big problems is that Iran can’t locate all the mines it laid during the war ). Trump’s decision to impose a Hormuz blockade risks triggering escalation (Trump warned that “ any Iranian who fires on us, or at peaceful vessels, will be blown to hell ” while Iran said any military vessels approaching the waterway would be considered a ceasefire violation ). According to a WSJ report Sunday night, the US could resume limited military strikes against Iran .
• How we’re thinking about the situation – the Pakistan meeting was definitely a disappointment for US equities (especially considering the recent rally and the fact the SPX had rebounded back to the pre-war resistance zone of ~6900-7000), but we don’t think it will trigger an immediate collapse of the ceasefire (which still seems to be holding as of Mon morning) or an escalation in fighting (our core view remains that the escalatory options available to Trump are so bad that they remain unlikely, which is why he decided to impose a Hormuz blockade rather than launch a fresh round of strikes and/or a ground troop deployment ). Agreement was apparently reached on certain points (which Trump acknowledged Sunday morning – “ the meeting went well...most points were agreed to ”), and neither side is ruling out further talks . Iran has made commitments in the recent past not to pursue a nuclear weapon (this was a cornerstone of the 2015 nuclear agreement) and it wouldn’t take much for it to do so again , while its enriched uranium seems largely inaccessible . The IDF seems to be observing a ceasefire in/around Beirut , and it’s possible the talks between Israel, Lebanon, and the US this week (scheduled to occur in Washington) could yield further progress (which could help the US-Iran relationship ).

Data/monetary policy recap from Friday morning

• What bulls were talking about : the Michigan sentiment report was bad , but 1) 98% of the interviews took place before the recent ceasefire announcement and 2) long-term inflation expectations (which the Fed cares about most) were fairly muted. The Mar CPI wasn’t as bad as feared , with the core CPI only accelerating 10bp to +2.6% (vs. the Street +2.7%).
• What bears were talking about : the US CPI for Mar wasn’t as bad as feared, but inflation is still running very hot at +3.3% headline and +2.6% core (and the core number will face further upside pressure in the months ahead as it will take some time for the recent energy price spike to seep further into the broader economy). The Michigan sentiment survey for Apr was net negative , with a sharp deterioration in Expectations (down >5 points M/M to 46.1) and a big short-term inflation jump (1-year inflation expectations rose 100bp to +4.8% vs. +3.8% in Mar). The inability of Treasuries to rally at all this week is a giant red flag about the inflationary implications of the war. San Francisco Fed President Daly warned in a Reuters interview that the Iran war-driven oil shock will make it even harder for US inflation to return to the 2% target (“We had work to do before we had the oil price shock; with the oil price shock, the work just takes longer”). China’s PPI had been a major source of global disinflation , but those days might be coming to an end based on the Mar data (the PPI flipped into positive territory for the first time in more than three years).
